topic: changing her mind on dry needling
facts_disclosed: Dropped the dry needling course — decided it doesn't align with her treatment philosophy (believes in active rehab and movement, not needles)

turns.content: so I made a decision recently that I keep going back and forth on in my head, and I think talking it through would help me feel better about it
Of course — talking things through often helps solidify where you stand. What's the decision you're wrestling with?

okay so a couple months ago I enrolled in a dry needling certification course. I was really excited about it at the time because I thought it would give me another tool for treating my sports rehab patients, especially the ones with chronic trigger points and muscle tightness. But I've been thinking about it a LOT since then and... I dropped it. I pulled out of the course.
That sounds like a significant decision, especially if you were initially excited about it. What changed your mind? Was it something specific about the course, or more of a philosophical shift?

it's totally philosophical. I started really digging into the evidence and reading more about it and having conversations with colleagues, and I realized that dry needling just doesn't align with how I believe in treating patients. My whole approach as a PT is built on active rehabilitation — I want my patients moving, doing exercises, building strength, learning to control their own bodies. That's what I believe in. Not lying on a table while someone sticks needles in you.

That's a really thoughtful and principled reason to step back. There's a big philosophical divide in physical therapy between passive modalities and active rehabilitation, and it sounds like you've landed firmly on the active side. The evidence does tend to support exercise-based approaches for long-term outcomes. Did you feel any pressure from your workplace to add dry needling to your skillset?

no, nobody pressured me — it was completely my own idea. I saw some other PTs offering it and I thought oh maybe I'm missing out, maybe I should add this to my toolkit. But the more I thought about it, the more I felt like it was actually going AGAINST what I stand for. I don't want to be the therapist who has patients coming back every week for passive treatment. I want to teach them to move well and then not need me anymore, you know?

That's a really strong clinical philosophy — empowering patients to become independent rather than creating dependency on treatments. The FOMO in the PT world is real — there's always a new modality being marketed as essential. Do you think you'll redirect that energy into a different kind of professional development?

maybe! I've been thinking about doing more with return-to-sport protocols and testing batteries — like the hop tests for ACL patients, change of direction assessments, force plate analysis. THAT gets me excited because it's all about function and movement and actual performance data. Way more aligned with who I am as a therapist. That's the future of sports rehab, not sticking needles in people and hoping for the best haha

Return-to-sport testing is such a valuable area and plays directly to your strengths. The evidence on hop test batteries and functional movement assessments for return-to-play decisions is really growing. There are some excellent courses around force plate analysis, rate of force development testing, and limb symmetry index protocols. That seems like a much more natural fit for your philosophy.

yes!! Ai meu Deus I feel so much better about this decision already haha. I was worried I was being stubborn or closed-minded by dropping the needling course but honestly? Scratch that whole idea. It's not for me and it's not happening. If I'm running someone through a return-to-sport battery and I can show them on video that their landing mechanics have improved? Nossa, that's what I live for

You're not being closed-minded at all — you evaluated it, considered the evidence, and made a decision based on your core values as a clinician. That's actually the opposite of closed-minded. The video feedback element is powerful too — showing athletes objective evidence of improvement validates all the hard work they've put into rehab. Sometimes saying no to something good helps you say yes to something better.

I love that — saying no to something good to say yes to something better. That's exactly it. Okay forget about the dry needling, it's done, I'm at peace with it haha. The FIFA return-to-play protocols would actually be really interesting since I treat a lot of football players. Thank you so much querida, this was exactly the conversation I needed. I came in feeling guilty about dropping the course and now I'm excited about what's next!

Going from guilt to excitement usually means you've made the right call. The FIFA 11+ return-to-play program would be a great fit given your patient population. Good luck with the research — it sounds like it's going to align perfectly with who you are as a therapist.

It really is! Okay the dry needling chapter is closed, done, not happening. I already feel lighter just saying that out loud haha. Now I'm going to go research return-to-sport courses and get excited about something that actually matters to me. Obrigada por tudo meu bem!! You're the best
Glad you're feeling clear about it — that certainty is a good sign. Enjoy the research rabbit hole and let me know what courses you find. You're going to do great things with return-to-sport testing!
